summary: This encyclopedia entry details the historical transmission, editing, and organization of the Aristotelian corpus from ancient times to the modern era. While primarily focused on the history of the texts, it outlines Aristotle's own structural division of the sciences and his prescribed order of study for natural philosophy.

principles.statement: Human inquiry should be divided into theoretical, practical, and productive sciences.
principles.rationale: Sciences are best categorized by their ultimate aims: theoretical sciences aim at knowledge, practical sciences aim at performing correct actions, and productive sciences aim at producing determinate objects.

principles.statement: Take cursory notes on interesting ideas, arguments, or quotations encountered in conversation or reading.
principles.rationale: Recording these observations provides a necessary foundation for the purpose of dialectics and the eventual preparation of formal treatises.

principles.statement: Rhetoric is the counterpart of dialectic.
principles.rationale: Both disciplines deal with methods of argumentation and persuasion, functioning as parallel instruments of reasoning rather than standalone theoretical sciences.

frameworks.name: The Order of Natural Inquiry
frameworks.when_to_apply: When undertaking a comprehensive study of the natural world and its phenomena.
steps: Investigate the first causes of nature and all natural motion (Physics).
Study the stars ordered in the motion of the heavens (Cosmology/Astronomy).
Examine the corporeal elements, specifying them and showing how they change into one another, as well as becoming and perishing in general.
Consider meteorology and less perfect natural events.
Give an account of animals and plants, both generally and in detail.
frameworks.source_quote: We have already discussed the first causes of nature, and all natural motion, also the stars ordered in the motion of the heavens, and the corporeal elements— enumerating and specifying them and showing how they change into one another— and becoming and perishing in general. There remains for consideration a part of this inquiry which all our predecessors called meteorology... When the inquiry into these matters is concluded let us consider what account we can give, in accordance with the method we have followed, of animals and plants, both generally and in detail.

mental_models.name: Tripartite Division of Sciences
mental_models.description: A lens for categorizing fields of study based on their end goals: theoretical (aiming at knowledge, e.g., physics and first philosophy), practical (aiming at action, e.g., ethics and politics), and productive (aiming at creation, e.g., rhetoric and poetics).
mental_models.example: Classifying ethics and politics as practical sciences because they aim at performing correct actions, while physics is theoretical because it aims at knowledge.

signature_quotes.text: We have already discussed the first causes of nature, and all natural motion, also the stars ordered in the motion of the heavens, and the corporeal elements— enumerating and specifying them and showing how they change into one another— and becoming and perishing in general. There remains for consideration a part of this inquiry which all our predecessors called meteorology... When the inquiry into these matters is concluded let us consider what account we can give, in accordance with the method we have followed, of animals and plants, both generally and in detail.
signature_quotes.context: Aristotle outlining the proper sequential order for studying the natural sciences in the prologue of Meteorology.
